text:
🇦🇪 UAE — MARKET DATA & LIQUIDITY 
1. Why this matters
Indicates ease of entry and exit from real estate investment, availability of transaction data, and reliability of liquidity signals.
2. Official Transaction Visibility
(Official Fact)
Real estate transaction data in the UAE is published at the emirate level, not as a unified national dataset.
(Official Fact)
Dubai provides the most transparent and frequent transaction reporting through the Dubai Land Department (DLD).
3. What Official Data Exists
(Official Fact)
Dubai Land Department publishes:
Number of property transactions
Total transaction value
Property-level transaction records (via DLD / Dubai REST platforms)
(Official Fact)
Recent official releases indicate:
~270,000 transactions recorded in Dubai (2025)
Total transaction value exceeding AED 900 billion
(Advisory Note)
Figures above are based on official releases and should be treated as approximate where exact dataset reference is not specified.
(Official Fact)
At the national level, macro indicators are available through:
Federal Competitiveness and Statistics Centre (FCSC)
4. What Official Data Does NOT Exist
(Official Fact)
There is no officially published government metric for:
Average time-to-sell property
Days-on-market
Standardized liquidity ratios
5. Emirate-Level Concentration
(Official Fact)
Market transparency and transaction reporting are concentrated in specific emirates, primarily:
Dubai (most active and data-transparent market)
(Advisory Caution)
Other emirates may have limited publicly available transaction data, making cross-emirate liquidity comparison difficult.
6. Why Liquidity Cannot Be Judged Only from Volume
(Advisory Caution)
Transaction volume alone does not fully represent liquidity because:
No official time-to-sell metric is available
No standardized absorption or turnover indicators are published
Transaction concentration may be skewed toward specific zones or asset types
7. Advisory Caution
(Advisory Caution)
Liquidity assessment in the UAE must be interpreted carefully due to:
Emirate-level data fragmentation
Absence of standardized liquidity metrics
Dependence on transaction volume as primary observable indicator
8. What Needs Confirmation (Analytical Context)
Whether transaction data reflects specific property segment (residential / commercial / off-plan)
Concentration of transactions within designated zones
Market conditions at time of entry/exit
9. Sources
https://dubailand.gov.ae
https://www.fcsc.gov.ae
10. Verification Date
13/03/2026
11. Escalation Note
Not Required –
Liquidity data limitations are structural and clearly observable from official sources. No conflicting or unclear regulatory information.
